sanskrit:
यथा प्रकाशयत्येकः कृत्स्नं लोकमिमं रविः |
क्षेत्रं क्षेत्री तथा कृत्स्नं प्रकाशयति भारत ॥३४॥
iast:
yathā prakāśayatyekaḥ kṛtsnaṃ lokamimṃ raviḥ |
kṣetraṃ kṣetrī tathā kṛtsnaṃ prakāśayati bhārata ||34||
explanation: Just as the one sun illumines this entire world, so does the Lord of the field (the Self) illumine the entire field, O Bharata. This famous analogy compares the Self to the sun — one consciousness illumines the entire body-mind field, just as one sun lights the whole world.
synthesis: Just as the one sun illumines the entire world, so the Lord of the field illumines the entire field. Shankara sees this as the clearest metaphor for consciousness: one awareness illumines all experience. Ramanuja reads it as God's one light manifesting as the consciousness of every soul. The Bhakti tradition meditates on Krishna as the inner sun of the heart, whose light reveals all truth. Madhva explains that the Self illumines the body as the sun illumines the world — by inherent luminous power — yet remains forever distinct from what it illumines. Abhinavagupta sees consciousness as the universal illuminator — pure prakasha that reveals all experiences without being any particular experience. Vallabha teaches that as one sun illumines all, one God illumines every field of experience — devotion is turning toward this inner sun. Tilak reads this as inspiration for leadership: the ideal karma yogi illumines their sphere of action with clarity and energy without entanglement. Vivekananda finds a profound image: you are the light, not the objects you illuminate — your awareness reveals everything without being diminished by what it reveals.

commentaries.text: Shankara explains that just as the sun illumines the world without being affected by what it illumines — shining equally on clean and unclean — the Self illumines all mental states without being affected by them. This is the nature of pure consciousness.

Ramanuja teaches that the Self, like the sun, illumines the body-field through its inherent consciousness. The soul's knowledge-nature makes all bodily experience possible, just as the sun's light makes all worldly activity possible.

The bhakti tradition sees this verse as pointing to the divine light within every being — the light by which all inner and outer experiences are known. This inner sun is God Himself, the light that never dims, present in every heart as the eternal illuminator.

Madhva explains that the Self illumines the body as the sun illumines the world — by its inherent luminous power. Just as the sun does not become the objects it illumines, the Self is forever distinct from the body it lights up. God, as the supreme Self, illumines all selves.

Abhinavagupta sees the sun metaphor as pointing to consciousness as the universal illuminator. Just as the sun reveals all forms without being any of them, pure awareness reveals all experiences — thoughts, emotions, perceptions — without being any particular experience. This is prakasha, the self-luminous nature of Shiva.

Vallabha teaches that as one sun illumines the whole world, one God illumines every field of experience. The Lord is the light of every consciousness, the illuminator of every soul. Devotion is turning toward this inner sun and basking in its rays.

Tilak reads this verse as inspiration for leadership: the ideal karma yogi illumines their sphere of action like the sun — bringing clarity, warmth, and energy to everything they touch, without becoming entangled in any single situation.

Vivekananda finds here a profound image of the Self's relationship to experience: you are the light, not the objects you illuminate. Your awareness is like the sun — it reveals everything but is not diminished or altered by what it reveals. This is the key to unshakeable identity.

applications.personal_growth: You are the light that illumines all your experiences — thoughts, emotions, sensations — without being any of them. Just as the sun reveals everything in its light without being changed, your awareness reveals your entire inner world without being altered.
applications.career_business: A great leader illumines the organization like the sun illumines the world — making everything visible, enabling all activity, yet not getting entangled in any particular function. Be the light that enables clarity rather than the hand that controls every detail.
applications.health: Your consciousness illumines your body's condition — you are aware of health and illness, pleasure and pain. But the awareness itself is not sick or healthy, just as the sun is not dark or light. This distinction brings peace amid physical challenges.
applications.relationships: In relationships, be the light that reveals rather than the force that controls. Illuminate the truth of the relationship — its strengths, challenges, and possibilities — without trying to manipulate what you see.

sanskrit:
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोरेवमन्तरं ज्ञानचक्षुषा |
भूतप्रकृतिमोक्षं च ये विदुर्यान्ति ते परम् ॥३५॥
iast:
kṣetrakṣetrajñayorevamantaraṃ jñānacakṣuṣā |
bhūtaprakṛtimokṣaṃ ca ye viduryānti te param ||35||
explanation: Those who, with the eye of knowledge, perceive the distinction between the field and the knower of the field, and also the liberation of beings from Prakriti — they attain the Supreme. This closing verse of Chapter 13 declares that those who discern the difference between matter and spirit, and understand how beings are freed from material nature, reach the highest goal.
synthesis: Those who perceive the distinction between the field and the knower of the field with the eye of knowledge, and the liberation of beings from Prakriti, go to the Supreme. Shankara sees this as the culminating promise of jnana yoga: discrimination between Self and not-Self leads to moksha. Ramanuja reads it as the soul's liberation through God's grace when it understands its true nature. The Bhakti tradition celebrates this as the devotee's graduation — the eye of knowledge reveals the path home to God. Madhva concludes that liberation comes through perceiving the real distinction with the scripture-informed eye of knowledge illumined by God's grace. Abhinavagupta sees the 'eye of knowledge' as the opening of the third eye — awareness that sees through the veil of body-identification, achieving liberating recognition. Vallabha teaches that this supreme goal is attained through God-given wisdom — ultimately a gift of divine grace. Tilak confirms that right knowledge leads to right action and ultimately to liberation — the karma yogi attains the goal while continuing to act. Vivekananda concludes that the eye of knowledge is available to all sincere seekers — distinguishing Self from body is the one realization that frees humanity.

commentaries.text: Shankara concludes that the entire chapter serves one purpose: to develop the 'eye of knowledge' that sees the eternal Self as distinct from the temporary field. Those who gain this vision are liberated from the beginningless cycle of Prakriti and attain the Supreme Brahman.

Ramanuja teaches that those who understand through knowledge and devotion the distinction between matter and soul, and the means by which the soul is freed from material bondage — through surrender to God — attain the supreme abode of the Lord.

The bhakti tradition concludes that the 'eye of knowledge' is ultimately the eye of love — for it is devotion that opens the inner vision capable of distinguishing the eternal from the temporary. Those who see with this loving wisdom reach God, the supreme destination.

Madhva concludes that liberation comes through perceiving the real distinction between kshetra and kshetrajna with the eye of knowledge. This 'eye of knowledge' is scripture-informed reasoning illumined by God's grace. The freed soul attains God's eternal abode.

Abhinavagupta sees the 'eye of knowledge' as the opening of the third eye — the eye of awareness that sees through the veil of identification with the body-field. Those who achieve this recognition (pratyabhijna) are liberated and attain the supreme nature of consciousness.

Vallabha teaches that the supreme goal — liberation from matter — is attained by those who see the difference between the field and its knower through God-given wisdom. This knowledge is ultimately a gift of divine grace (pushti), not merely human effort.

Tilak sees this final verse as confirming that right knowledge leads to right action and ultimately to liberation. The karma yogi who understands the distinction between Self and not-Self while continuing to act in the world attains the supreme goal.

Vivekananda concludes that the 'eye of knowledge' is available to all who sincerely seek it. Distinguishing the Self from the body — the knower from the field — is the one realization that frees humanity. This chapter's teaching is the essence of all Vedantic education.

applications.personal_growth: The ultimate goal of all self-knowledge is developing the inner eye that sees your true nature as distinct from your body, mind, and circumstances. This clear seeing is not intellectual but experiential — and it sets you free.
applications.career_business: Developing the ability to see the essential versus the temporary in business — distinguishing enduring principles from transient trends, core identity from passing roles — is the mark of true professional wisdom.
applications.health: The ultimate health wisdom is seeing yourself as more than your body while still caring for it completely. This paradoxical vision — I am not this body, yet I honor this body — is the foundation of peaceful, empowered health management.
applications.relationships: The deepest relationship wisdom is seeing your partner as a soul temporarily inhabiting a body, while also fully honoring and engaging with the human being in front of you. This is love with the eye of knowledge.
